name: Enpass
tagline: Best offline-first password manager with cloud flexibility
description: Enpass stores your data locally by default and lets you choose your own cloud storage, giving you complete control over where your passwords live.
longDescription: Enpass takes a fundamentally different approach to password management. Instead of storing your data on company servers, Enpass stores everything locally on your device and gives you the option to sync via your own cloud storage (iCloud, Dropbox, Google Drive, OneDrive, or WebDAV). This offline-first architecture means you have complete control over your data, and there is no central server to breach. The trade-off is that you are responsible for your own sync setup and backups. The interface is clean and functional, with good desktop apps for Windows, Mac, and Linux. A one-time purchase option is available alongside a subscription model.

keyFeatures.name: Offline-First Architecture
keyFeatures.description: All data stored locally on your device by default. No central server to breach. Sync via your own iCloud, Dropbox, Google Drive, OneDrive, or WebDAV account.

keyFeatures.name: Multiple Vaults
keyFeatures.description: Create separate vaults for work, personal, and family. Each vault can sync to different cloud accounts. Complete data segregation.

keyFeatures.name: One-Time Purchase
keyFeatures.description: Buy once and use forever. One-time license includes all features and 12 months of updates. Subscription also available at $1.99/month.

keyFeatures.name: Linux Support
keyFeatures.description: Full-featured native Linux desktop application. One of the few password managers with first-class Linux support.

specs.Sync Options: iCloud, Dropbox, Google Drive, OneDrive, WebDAV

verdict: Enpass is the best choice for users who want complete control over their password data and are comfortable managing their own sync and backups. The offline-first architecture eliminates the risk of a centralized breach, and the one-time purchase option is great for subscription-averse users. The trade-off is more setup work and less convenience.

faqs.question: Is Enpass safe if I sync via Dropbox?
faqs.answer: Yes. Enpass encrypts your data before it leaves your device. Even if someone accessed your Dropbox account, they would find only encrypted blobs that cannot be decrypted without your master password. Enpass never has access to your data or encryption keys.

faqs.question: Is the one-time purchase better than subscription?
faqs.answer: For long-term users, the one-time purchase ($79.99) is better value than the subscription ($1.99/month). After about 3.5 years, the one-time purchase pays for itself. However, note that the one-time license includes 12 months of updates — continued updates may require renewal.

author.name: Alex Chen
author.title: Senior Cybersecurity Analyst
author.bio: Alex has tested over 30 password managers across 5 years.
